coredata.dc:title: Argyreia nervosa-derived carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective epinephrine detection

Park, Tae Jung
coredata.dc:description: This paper explores the utilization of Argyreia nervosa as a precursor for synthesizing carbon dots (AN-CDs) and their potential utilization as a fluorescent chemical sensor for specific epinephrine detection. The synthesis methodology presents several advantages, including a straightforward, single-step green process, exceptional stability, and an inherent biocompatibility of the resulting carbon dots. The synthesized AN-CDs exhibit a distinctive fluorescence emission maximum at 430 nm in the blue region upon excitation at 340 nm. A linear quenching of fluorescence intensity is observed upon the addition of epinephrine, enabling a straightforward analytical method for epinephrine detection. The probe proves high specificity towards epinephrine, despite the presence of common interfering substances. The practical utility is evidenced by its successful application in detecting epinephrine in pharmaceutical and serum samples, demonstrating its potential for biomedical and environmental monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Epinephrine
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Argyreia Nervosa
Spectroscopic techniques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: . Preparation of powder of Argyreia nervosa and synthesis of AN-CDs
Sensing of epinephrine in pharmaceutical and serum samples
Fluorescence assay of epinephrine using AN-CDs as a probe
Real sample analysis using AN-CDs as a probe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Chung-Ang University Young Scientist Research Scholarship in 2022. RHS thanks the Director, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat for allowing the internship programme.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra (λex: 340 nm, λem: 430 nm) and UV–vis spectra of CDs from Argyreia nervosa leaves. The inset image corresponds to the AN-CDs solution in daylight and under a UV lamp at 365 nm.
XPS spectra of CDs. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) high-resolution spectra of C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s. (e) XRD spectrum of CDs derived from A. nervosa leaves.

FE-TEM images of AN-CDs at (a) 20 nm, inset: histogram plotted at 20 nm scale bar, (b) 10 nm scale bar, and (c) 5 nm scale bar. FE-TEM image of AN-CDs with epinephrine at (d) 20 nm, inset: histogram plotted at 20 nm scale bar, (e) 10 nm scale bar, and (f) 5 nm scale bar.

(a) Fluorescence intensities of CDs with various biomarkers at 1 mM (1. Bovine serum albumin, 2. Dehydroepiandrosterone, 3. Se-Methyl selenocysteine, 4. Dopamine, 5. Cortisone, 6. Creatinine, 7. DL-Normetanephrine hydrochloride, 8. Epinephrine, 9. Uric acid, 10. γ-aminobutyric acid, 11. Methyl nicotinate, 12. Bilirubin) (λex: 340 nm, λem: 430 nm). (b) Photographic image of fluorescence intensities of AN-CDs after the addition of biomarkers under UV-lamp at 365 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with the addition of different concentrations of epinephrine ranging with 0.05–1,000 µM (λex: 340 nm, λem: 430 nm), (b) the fluorescence quenching under UV light at 365 nm. (c) Calibration curve constructed between I0/I and different concentrations of epinephrine (0.05–1000 µM).
Synthesis of AN-CDs using Argyreia nervosa leaves extract as a carbon source.
Comparison of the analytical characteristics of AN-CDs-based fluorescent sensor with the other reported analytical methods for the assay of epinephrine.

row.entry: Nanocomposite of ZnO NPs and MWCNTs

head.ce:title: Argyreia nervosa-derived carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective epinephrine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok–ro, Dongjak–gu, Seoul 06974 Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok–ro, Dongjak–gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007 Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Daegu Center, Korea Basic Science Institute, 80 Daehak-ro, Buk-gu, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Daegu Center, Korea Basic Science Institute, 80 Daehak-ro, Buk-gu, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sustainable method was utilized to synthesize blue, fluorescent carbon dots from Argyreia nervosa (AN-CDs) and applied as a probe for detecting epinephrine through a fluorescence quenching mechanism.

This paper explores the utilization of Argyreia nervosa as a precursor for synthesizing carbon dots (AN-CDs) and their potential utilization as a fluorescent chemical sensor for specific epinephrine detection. The synthesis methodology presents several advantages, including a straightforward, single-step green process, exceptional stability, and an inherent biocompatibility of the resulting carbon dots. The synthesized AN-CDs exhibit a distinctive fluorescence emission maximum at 430 nm in the blue region upon excitation at 340 nm. A linear quenching of fluorescence intensity is observed upon the addition of epinephrine, enabling a straightforward analytical method for epinephrine detection. The probe proves high specificity towards epinephrine, despite the presence of common interfering substances. The practical utility is evidenced by its successful application in detecting epinephrine in pharmaceutical and serum samples, demonstrating its potential for biomedical and environmental monitoring applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Green synthesis of nanostructure materials in analytical chemistry holds immense importance due to its significant environmental, economic, and societal benefits [1–4]. It minimizes environmental impact by reducing hazardous reagents and toxic solvents, which offers to establish a sustainable analytical approach [5–7]. It promotes resource efficiency by using renewable starting eco-friendly materials and solvents, conserving resources, and minimizing waste [8–10]. Green synthesis can be operated under milder conditions, lowering energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions, favoring to fabrication of numerous nanostructure materials [11–13]. In chemical analysis, eco-friendly nanomaterials-based optical probes play a key role in identifying various trace-level chemical species [14–16]. Argyreia nervosa (A. nervosa) is a medicinal plant having numerous pharmacological properties, including antimicrobial, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, antiulcer, immunomodulatory, hypoglycemic, and anticonvulsant [17,18].

A. nervosa, also known as the elephant creeper or woolly morning glory, is a long-lasting climbing plant Indigenous to the Indian subcontinent [19,20]. Importantly, it has garnered significant attention in synthesizing nanomaterials due to its abundant phytochemical composition [21]. A. nervosa contains a variety of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and polyphenols, which can act as natural, and capping agents in the eco-friendly synthesis of nanomaterials [22,23]. The above information on A. nervosa highlights the rationale for exploring A. nervosa as a potential precursor for the green synthesis of carbon dots, which have numerous applications in bioimaging, sensing, catalysis, and optoelectronics [24]. The fluorescence properties of carbon dots make them highly attractive for the detection of various target molecular and ionic species in biofluids and environmental samples, offering a miniaturized sensitive and efficient analytical approach for the recognition of target analytes. Red-emitting carbon dots have been explored as fluorescent probes for the detection of quinolone antibiotics with an impressive limit of detection (LOD) [25]. Additionally, phenylboric acid-functionalized carbon dots have shown rapid and effective fluorescent sensing of glucose in serum [26]. Moreover, utilizing A. nervosa as a precursor for carbon dot synthesis offers a sustainable and cost-effective alternative to conventional chemical routes [27,28]. This innovative method not only improves the value of A. nervosa but also associates with green chemistry principles, promoting the use of renewable resources in the development of advanced materials [29,30]. By leveraging the intrinsic properties of this versatile plant, the present work aims to create green carbon dots that can significantly impact sensing biological important molecules. Further, no studies have yet reported the synthesis of carbon dots using A. nervosa leaf powder as a carbon source.

Stress is a prevalent aspect of contemporary life and can negatively impact both physical and mental well-being. It can influence the hippocampus, the brain region crucial for memory and learning [31,32]. Hence, it is essential to routinely assess stress levels. Epinephrine, also known as adrenaline, plays an essential role in managing the body's reaction to stress and emergency situations [33,34]. It is a hormone and neurotransmitter that initiates the body's fight-or-flight response, which includes increasing heart rate and dilating airways [35–37]. The precise detection of epinephrine is vital in various medical diagnostics, monitoring stress levels, and managing conditions like asthma and allergies. Traditional methods for detecting epinephrine often involve complex laboratory techniques [38,39]. However, recent advancements in nanomaterials-based sensing technology, specifically fluorescent nanosensor, have revolutionized in the field of bioanalytical chemistry, thereby improving the selectivity and sensitivity of analytical techniques for chemical analysis [40,41]. In view of this, several reports have documented the detection of epinephrine using various nanomaterials. For instance, molybdenum oxide quantum dots have been utilized as a fluorescent probe for epinephrine detection through a turn OFF-ON-OFF mechanism [42]. The fluorescence of boron carbon nitride nanosheets was quenched by epinephrine, offering to detect epinephrine with greater selectivity [34]. Furthermore, a colorimetric biosensor was engineered for non-tagged epinephrine detection, utilizing flower-like biomimetic copper sulfide antioxidant nanozymes [43]. Fluorescent molybdenum nanoclusters have also been utilized as a sensing probe for epinephrine detection [44]. Precise detection of epinephrine was achieved using cucurbituril and a terbium metal–organic framework [45]. An integrated optical fiber with polydopamine-grafted amino silane-functionalized graphene oxide demonstrated a minimum LOD of 0.07 µM for epinephrine [46]. An electrochemical sensor was developed for sensitive and selective epinephrine detection using ZnO nanoparticles incorporated multi-walled carbon nanotubes hybrid nanomaterials [47]. Moreover, an enzyme-like activity of gold nanoclusters has been employed in colorimetric sensing of epinephrine [48]. These developed approaches not only increase the accuracy and rapidity for the detection of epinephrine but also show potential for future applications in biomedical analytical chemistry for real-time health monitoring. However, the present study focuses on the development of green analytical method for detecting epinephrine in pharmaceutical and serum samples using A. nervosa-derived carbon dots (AN-CDs) as a fluorescent probe.

In the present study, A. nervosa is utilized as a precursor for synthesizing carbon dots (AN-CDs) (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared fluorescent AN-CDs serve as a robust fluorescent probe for selective epinephrine detection. The methodology offers notable advantages, including a facile single-step green synthesis route, marked stability, and inherent biocompatibility. The synthesized carbon dots exhibit an emission maximum at 430 nm when excited at 340 nm. Upon the introduction of epinephrine to AN-CDs, a linear quenching in the fluorescence intensity of AN-CDs is observed, enabling to achievement of an impressive LOD of 37.04 nM for epinephrine detection. This probe exhibits notable specificity towards epinephrine amidst common interfering chemical species. Furthermore, the practical utility of AN-CDs is successfully demonstrated by assaying epinephrine in pharmaceutical and serum samples.

A. nervosa leaf powder was acquired as gift samples from Navsari Agriculture University (Surat, Gujarat, India). CaCl2, Cu(NO3)2, Na2CO3, Na2SO3, HCl and ethanol were purchased from SRL Chemicals. Epinephrine, bovine serum albumin, dehydroepiandrosterone, Se-(methyl) selenocysteine, dopamine, cortisone, creatinine, DL-normetanephrine HCl, uric acid, γ-aminobutyric acid, methyl nicotinate, cortisol, serotonin HCl and bilirubin, methionine, glutamic acid, histidine, cysteine, tryptophan, L-lysine, asparagine, alanine, FeSO4, Co(NO3)2, FeCl3, KBr, AlCl3, ZnSO4, Mg(NO3)2, Mn(NO3)2, H3PO4 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All solvents and chemicals employed in the analysis were of analytical grade. Milli-Q purified water was used for the entire research.

The fabricated AN-CDs from A. nervosa were assessed using multiple analytical techniques. Emission and excitation spectra were acquired using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, Largo, FL, USA) was utilized for UV–Vis absorption analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry (ALPHA II, Bruker, Germany) was used to confirm the surface functionality of AN-CDs. Elemental composition was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al K Alpha radiation, employing a Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed by using a New D8-Advance analyzer (Bruker AXS, Berlin, Germany) to identify the phase. The morphological and size-related properties of the synthesized AN-CDs were investigated by zeta potential (Horiba scientific SZ-100 V) and field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) model-JEM-F200 of JEOL, Tokyo, Japan. Fluorescence lifetime decay was measured using a fluorescence confocal microscopy (Microtime-200, Picoquant, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: . Preparation of powder of Argyreia nervosa and synthesis of AN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: All glassware underwent a rigorous cleaning process using acid solution and detergent followed by thorough rinsing with distilled water and acetone before utilization. The method for synthesis of AN-CDs is based on the procedure described in the literature [49–51]. Herein, we present the first synthesis of AN-CDs by employing A. nervosa as the carbonaceous precursor. The foliage of A. nervosa underwent initial cleansing with tap water, followed by a subsequent rinsing with distilled water, and desiccation within an oven. Post-desiccation, the botanical material was comminuted into finer particles utilizing a grinder. Then, 0.5 g of A. nervosa powder was combined with 2 ml of a 1:1 EtOH:H2O (v/v). The obtained mixture was heated to 80 °C until a blackish-brown color appeared, indicating successful carbonization. Subsequently, 10 ml of deionized water was used to suspend the carbonized powder and subjected to purification over six hours using a dialysis bag to obtain purified AN-CDs. The purified AN-CDs were kept for further usage at 4 °C.

For the detection of epinephrine, 1.0 mL of AN-CDs was integrated with 0.5 mL of various biomarker solutions, including bovine serum albumin, dehydroepiandrosterone, Se-(methyl) selenocysteine, dopamine, cortisone, creatinine, DL-normetanephrine HCl, uric acid, γ-aminobutyric acid, epinephrine, methyl nicotinate, and bilirubin. The fluorescence emission spectra of all samples were measured using an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The blue fluorescence of AN-CDs was quenched by 90 % only in the presence of epinephrine, while no significant fluorescence reduction was observed with the other biomarkers. This indicates that the synthesized AN-CDs can serve as a potential chemical sensor for epinephrine detection.

The quantum yield (QY) of AN-CDs was calculated with reference to quinine sulfate and determined using the following formula;
QY = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2

Here, the subscript “R” refers to the referenced quinine sulfate fluorescent dye, while “n” denotes the refractive index. “A” represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and “I” corresponds to the integrated emission intensity or the area under the emission curve for the sample. Using this method, the QY of AN-CDs was determined to be 22.3 % [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of epinephrine in pharmaceutical and serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the operational functionality of the probe, AN-CDs-based fluorescent probe was employed to detect epinephrine in pharmaceutical formulations. The epinephrine Injection of Rathi Laboratories (Hindustan) Pvt. Ltd. with a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 was collected from the local medical center in Surat, Gujarat, India. A serum sample was collected from Celix Laboratory, Surat, India. The collected serum sample was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm which helps to separate insoluble solids in serum and diluted at 1000-fold. Different concentrations (5, 2.5, and 1 µM) of epinephrine were spiked into serum samples and then analyzed using the aforementioned procedure.

The A. nervosa-derived carbon dots were characterized using a range of analytical techniques including UV–visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR, XPS, XRD, and HR-TEM. To optimize the synthesis conditions, different parameters such as reaction times (3, 6, 9 and 12 min) and temperatures (40, 60, 80 and 100 °C) were systematically varied. As time and temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity initially increased, suggesting that the carbonization of A. nervosa leaves powder led to the formation of carbon dots. However, at 100 °C of reaction temperature, a higher degree of carbonization occurred, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1a). It is also decreased for 12 min of reaction time due to excessive carbonization (Fig. S1b), which leads to form larger carbon particles. The highest fluorescence emission intensity was noticed at 9 min at 80 °C (Fig. S1a), indicating the optimal condition for the synthesis of AN-CDs. Along with this, to confirm carbonization, a control experiment was conducted by synthesizing AN-CDs using both the hydrothermal method and with and without A. nervosa powder. The fluorescence spectra revealed that AN-CDs synthesized using the heating mantle exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity compared to the hydrothermal method and the precursor-free synthesis. This result confirms the optimal carbonization achieved with the heating mantle method (Fig. S2a). Moreover, UV–Visible spectra confirmed the successful formation of AN-CDs synthesized using both the heating mantle and hydrothermal methods, as shown in Fig. S2b. The developed method provides a more efficient and optimized fabrication of AN-CDs as compared to widely used methods like hydrothermal, achieving enhanced fluorescence intensity and precise carbonization control. The emission spectra of AN-CDs were measured over a range of excitation wavelengths (λEx) from 300 to 400 nm to determine the highest emission wavelength (λEm). The optimal excitation wavelength for AN-CDs was determined to be 340 nm, as the highest emission was recorded at 430 nm (Fig. S3). The fabricated AN-CDs emit blue fluorescence upon exposure to a UV radiation lamp at 365 nm. Fig. 1 illustrates that the AN-CDs displayed broad absorption bands within the 275 to 400 nm range in their UV–vis absorption spectrum. Notably, there was a significant absorption peak at around 319 nm, a common feature among fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. This peak is due to the π–π* electronic transition in the AN-CDs, which occurs because the surface states trap excited state energy, resulting in strong fluorescence. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra exhibited a narrow emission spectrum, displaying a distinct peak at 430 nm upon excitation at 340 nm. Under normal daylight, the AN-CDs solution appeared light yellow (Inset image of Fig. 1). However, under UV light at 365 nm, it emitted a vivid blue fluorescence, clearly demonstrating the strong blue fluorescence from AN-CDs. The emission spectra of AN-CDs were monitored for 1 to 20 days to assess their stability. The fluorescence intensity remained unchanged throughout this period (Fig. S4), indicating that AN-CDs could serve as a potential fluorescent sensor for sensing epinephrine. The quantum yield of the AN-CDs was measured at 22.3 %, using quinine sulfate as the calibration standard.

FT-IR spectroscopic study was conducted to identify the surface functional groups of Argyreia nervosa and AN-CDs. The FTIR spectrum of A. nervosa leaves and AN-CDs (Fig. S5a and b) reveals distinct absorption bands characteristic of various functional groups. A broad peak around 3340 cm−1 indicates hydroxyl groups, typical of alcohols and phenols, in both A. nervosa leaves and AN-CDs. The peak intensity of the hydroxyl group was reduced in the case of AN-CDs. The C-H stretching vibrations of alkanes in A. nervosa leaves are indicated by peaks observed at 2923 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1. However, in the case of AN-CDs, these peaks show reduced intensity, indicating a change in aliphatic content. The peaks at 1720 and 1051 cm−1 signify the presence of carbonyl groups, suggesting the presence of phytochemicals in A. nervosa leaves, while the carbonyl peak in AN-CDs is reduced and shifted, reflecting changes in the chemical environment of C=O groups due to carbon dot formation [53,54]. These findings indicate that the successful synthesis of AN-CDs from A. nervosa leaves.

The XPS analysis was performed to investigate the AN-CDs elemental composition as well as electronic states (Fig. 2 a-d). In the XPS survey spectrum of AN-CDs, peaks corresponding to the elements C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s were noticed at 287.50 eV, 402.02 eV, and 534.02 eV, respectively, indicating an elemental composition of 68.07 % C, 30 % O, and 1.93 % N. Additionally, the binding energy peaks for C=C, C–N, and C=O bonds were observed at 284.84, 291.46 and 296.18 eV, respectively. XPS data validate the FTIR findings, providing confirmatory evidence of the organic groups present in the prepared AN-CDs.

To confirm the phase of AN-CDs, XRD analysis was performed (Fig. 2e). The XRD spectra of AN-CDs was confirmed the crystalline nature of AN-CDs, showing sharp peaks at the (002), (101), (102), and (103) planes, which suggest the existence of graphitic carbon. The calculated d-spacing values from these peaks were consistent with those of graphite lattice planes, further supporting the crystalline structure of AN-CDs. The structure of the AN-CDs was analyzed using FE-TEM analysis (Fig. 3 a-c). FE-TEM results showed that the AN-CDs are spherical with a uniform distribution and an average size of 3.01 ± 0.47 nm. The observed d-lattice spacing from the FE-TEM images corresponds to the graphite lattice plane, confirming the crystalline structure observed in the XRD results. Additionally, the FE-TEM analysis of AN-CDs in the presence of epinephrine exhibits that epinephrine causes the aggregation of AN-CDs (Fig. 3d-f). Following this, zeta potential analysis was conducted to determine the surface charge of the AN-CDs (Fig. S6). The AN-CDs exhibited a negative charge of − 25.65 mV indicating high stability of synthesized AN-CDs (Fig. S6b). Upon the addition of epinephrine, aggregation occurred, which transformed the surface charge from − 25.65 mV to − 7.69 mV (Fig. S6c).

The fluorescence response of the as-synthesized AN-CDs to varying pH levels in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), ranging from 2.0 to 12.0, was investigated by examining their emission spectra. This study included measurements with and without epinephrine to assess the effect of pH on the sensing ability of AN-CDs. In the absence of epinephrine, the emission spectra of the AN-CDs showed a decrease as the pH shifted towards acidic values, revealing the pH-dependent response of AN-CDs (Fig. S7a). Noticeably, the fluorescence of the AN-CDs decreased at every pH level in the presence of epinephrine across PBS pH values varying from 2.0 to 12.0. (Fig. S7b in). This indicates that AN-CDs can serve as an effective fluorescent sensor for detecting epinephrine without the need for optimizing buffer pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of epinephrine using AN-CDs as a probe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential of the synthesized AN-CDs as a simple fluorescent probe, the detection capability of AN-CDs was tested in the presence of various biomarkers, including bovine serum albumin, dehydroepiandrosterone, Se-(methyl) selenocysteine, dopamine, cortisone, creatinine, DL-normetanephrine HCl, uric acid, γ-aminobutyric acid, epinephrine, methyl nicotinate, and bilirubin (all at 1 mM). Fig. 4 a illustrates the changes in the emission spectral data of AN-CDs after adding the above biomarkers. Significant spectral changes were observed specifically when AN-CDs were exposed to epinephrine. The emission signal of AN-CDs was noticeably lowered in the presence of epinephrine, with a 90 % quenching of their fluorescence intensity. Additionally, the fluorescent nature of AN-CDs was visibly altered by epinephrine, changing from blue to colorless under a UV lamp set to 365 nm (Fig. 4b). These results indicate that the synthesized AN-CDs can qualitatively detect epinephrine through fluorescence quenching, demonstrating their potential as a fluorescent nanosensor for epinephrine sensing.

The mechanism of epinephrine sensing using AN-CDs as a fluorescent sensor was investigated through fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR, FE-TEM, fluorescence lifetime measurements, and zeta potential analysis of AN-CDs both without and with epinephrine. The quenching of AN-CDs fluorescence upon the addition of epinephrine may be attributed to the interaction between the two substances. FE-TEM analysis revealed that the synthesized AN-CDs discovered the development of a non-emissive complex following the introduction of epinephrine [55]. This aggregation increased the average size of the AN-CDs to 10.27 ± 1.49 nm, as shown in the FE-TEM images (Fig. 3d-f). FT-IR spectra were recorded as proof of the sensing mechanism of epinephrine sensing using AN-CDs (see Fig. S5b). The comparative FT-IR analysis of AN-CDs and AN-CDs with epinephrine illustrates the interaction between epinephrine and AN-CDs. The FT-IR results confirmed the strengthening in the characteristic peaks of stretching of –OH, stretching of C=O, bending of –C=C, and bending of –C–H in AN-CDs by the addition of epinephrine. A notable increase in the peak intensity at 3419 and 1611 cm−1, attributed to stretching of –OH and –C=O respectively, was observed in the AN-CDs + epinephrine spectrum. This indicates the strong hydrogen bonding between epinephrine and functional groups of AN-CDs [56]. Furthermore, the sharp peaks associated with stretching of –C–H at 2924 cm−1, stretching of –C=O at 1113 cm−1, bending of –C–H at 869 cm−1, and stretching of –C=C at 672 cm−1 were reduced. This reduction suggests the integration of surface functional groups of AN-CDs with epinephrine, resulting the aggregation of AN-CDs which leads a drastic quench in the emission intensity of AN-CDs.

The surface charge of the synthesized AN-CDs was determined as −25.65 mV, reflecting high dispersibility and stability. However, upon the addition of epinephrine, the zeta potential shifted to −7.69 mV (Fig. S6c), suggesting electrostatic interactions between the AN-CDs and epinephrine[57]. The fluorescence lifetime assessment of AN-CDs and AN-CDs in conjugation with epinephrine revealed that the detection of epinephrine through static quenching as AN-CDs lifetime decreased from 3.41 ns to 3.2 ns (Fig. S8) [58].

The investigation focused on AN-CDs ability to selectively differentiate epinephrine from a group of biomarkers, including bovine serum albumin, dehydroepiandrosterone, Se-methyl selenocysteine, dopamine, cortisone, creatinine, DL-normetanephrine HCl, uric acid, γ-aminobutyric acid, methyl nicotinate, and bilirubin. It was noted that the other biomarkers did not induce fluorescence enhancement or quenching. However, selective fluorescence quenching was observed in the presence of epinephrine, even among other biomarkers. The spectral data indicates that epinephrine demonstrated high selectivity among various biomarkers. To assess the selectivity of AN-CDs for epinephrine against other chemical species, the fluorescence spectra of AN-CDs at 430 nm were recorded in the presence of various interfering substances, including metal ions (Zn2+, Al3+, Mg2+ and SO3 2-, PO4 3-, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, NO3 –, CO3 -2, Cl-, Ca2+, K+), biomolecules (histidine, cysteine, tryptophan, L-lysine, asparagine, alanine, methionine and glutamic acid), and certain biomarkers (methyl nicotinate, cortisol, serotonin HCl), both with and without epinephrine. Figs. S9a-b reveal that fluorescence quenching of AN-CDs was not observed with any of the interference substances tested, except in the presence of epinephrine. This suggests that AN-CDs could serve as selective fluorescent nanosensor for detecting epinephrine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis using AN-CDs as a probe
ce:section.ce:para: After assessing the sensitivity and specificity of AN-CDs for detecting epinephrine, their practical utility was examined for quantification of epinephrine in pharmaceutical and spiked human serum samples, where epinephrine levels are frequently observed. A vial of adrenaline injection containing 1 mg/mL of epinephrine manufactured by Rathi Laboratories (Hindustan) Pvt. Ltd., India. Since the adrenaline injection contained epinephrine at 1 mg mL−1 concentration, various concentrations of 1.0, 2.5, and 5.0 μM of epinephrine were prepared and quantified using an AN-CDs-based sensor. For blood samples, a 1000-fold dilution was performed, and epinephrine concentrations of 1.0, 2.5, and 5.0 μM were spiked. Following sample preparation, significant spectral changes were observed. The recovery of epinephrine in pharmaceutical samples and serum samples ranged from 97.88 % to 99.05 % and 99.57 % to 99.58 %, respectively, through relative standard deviations (RSD) varying from 1.42 % to 1.74 % and 0.84 % to 1.41 %, as detailed in Table S1. These results suggest that AN-CDs are highly reliable and accurate for the detection of epinephrine in both serum and pharmaceutical samples. Furthermore, the developed AN-CDs-based fluorescent probe is inexpensive since the total cost for epinephrine analysis is ∼₩2150 KRW ($1.46).

In summary, a new and efficient method was introduced for the preparation of A. nervosa-mediated carbon dots using a heating mantle. The synthesized AN-CDs exhibited a pronounced blue emission at 430 nm upon excitation at 340 nm, achieving a quantum yield of 22.3 %. FT-IR analysis and XPS analysis determined that the surfaces of AN-CDs are primarily composed of fundamental elements such as carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. Additionally, XRD analysis confirmed that the synthesized AN-CDs possess a crystalline structure. The AN-CDs demonstrated effective dispersion in an aqueous medium, exhibiting an average size of 3.41 ± 0.53 nm. The AN-CDs functioned as a simple fluorescent nanoprobe to detect epinephrine through a static quenching mechanism. The prepared AN-CDs-based fluorescent nanosensor exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity, achieving a LOD of 37.04 nM within a linear concentration range of 0.05 to 50 μM for epinephrine. Interference studies with various metal ions, biomolecules, and biomarkers confirmed the selective nature of the AN-CDs towards epinephrine. Assaying of epinephrine in pharmaceutical and spiked human serum samples was effectively completed by using AN-CDs as a probe. The as-synthesized probe offers several advantages, including sensitivity, selectivity, cost-effectiveness and rapid response for epinephrine assay. Thus, the as-synthesized AN-CDs open new avenues for the development of facile analytical tools for epinephrine detection in pharmaceutical and human serum samples.

Human serum sample was collected with informed written consent from the patients by Celix Laboratory in Surat, Gujarat, India, and approved by the Ethics Committee of The Indian Council of Medical Research, India.

Rohini Shinde: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Juhi Bhadresh Raval: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis. Madhura Deshpande: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Eunji Shin: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Weon-Sik Chae: Formal analysis. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Tae Jung Park: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Chung-Ang University Young Scientist Research Scholarship in 2022. RHS thanks the Director, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat for allowing the internship programme.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2025.113976.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. M. Revabhai, T. J. Park, S. K. Kailasa, “One-step hydrothermal approach for synthesis of hydroxy functionalized boron nitride nanosheets for fluorescence detection of uric acid in biological samples,” Inorg. Chem. Commun. 148 (2023), Article 110346, doi: 10.1016/j.inoche.2022.110346.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. C. Kishore, S. Perumal, R. Atchudan, T. N. J. I. Edison, A. K. Sundramoorthy, S. Sangaraju Y. R. Lee, “Eco-friendly synthesis of functionalized carbon nanodots from cashew nut skin waste for bioimaging,” Catalysts 13 (2023), Article 547, doi: 10.3390/catal13030547.

Lee, “Eco-friendly Coccinia grandis-derived excitation-dependent fluorescence of carbon dots for In-Vivo bioimaging and fluorescent ink applications,” Mater. Sci. Eng. B 303 (2024), Article 117300, doi: 10.1016/j.mseb.2024.117300.

Technol. 4 (2024), pp. 272–281, doi: 10.1021/acsfoodscitech.3c00539.

Commun. 171 (2025), Article 113660, doi: 10.1016/j.inoche.2024.113660.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive detection of epinephrine in human serum via fluorescence enhancement of gold nanoclusters
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Govindaraju, A. S. Reddy, J. Kim, K. Yun, “Sensitive detection of epinephrine in human serum via fluorescence enhancement of gold nanoclusters,” Appl. Surf. Sci. 498 (2019), Article 143837, doi: 10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.143837.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: LaSrO3 perovskite quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for the detection of bilirubin and epinephrine via FRET and IFE mechanisms
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. P. Makwana, M. P. Deshpande, N. I. Malek, T. J. Park, S. K. Kailasa, “LaSrO3 perovskite quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for the detection of bilirubin and epinephrine via FRET and IFE mechanisms,” J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem. 461 (2025), Article 116172, doi: 10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.116172.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rohini Shinde received her BS degree in School of Nanoscience Technology from Shivaji University in 2021. Now, she is studying MS leading to PhD course in Chung-Ang University since 2022. Her research interests focus on synthesis of nanomaterials, and their applications for detection of environmental contaminants and harmful chemicals.

Juhi Bhadresh Raval is a doctoral student in the Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat. She completed her BSc in Biotechnology from Uka Tarsadia University in 2015 and obtained her post-graduate degree from Sardar Patel University, in 2020. At present, she is working as a Research Scholar in the Functional Nanomaterial Laboratory under the supervision of Dr. Suresh Kumar Kailasa. She has published 2 research publications, 2 review articles, and 6 book chapters in internationally recognized journals. She is a life member of the Association of Environmental Analytical Chemistry of India (AEACI) and society of material chemistry India (SMC).

Madhura Deshpande received her MS degree in School of Nanoscience Technology from Shivaji University in 2019. Now, she is studying Ph.D. course in Chung-Ang University since 2022. Her research interests focus on synthesis of nanomaterials and application for drug delivery carrier in vitro and in vivo.

Eunji Shin received her BS degree in chemistry department from Chung-Ang University in 2023. Now, she is studying MS course in Chung-Ang University since 2023. Her research interests focus on synthesis of organic compounds and nanomaterials, and their applications for detection of environmental contaminants and harmful chemicals.

Weon-Sik Chae his Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Yonsei University in 2003. After a research associate position at Yonsei University (2003-2005), he joined University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (2005-2007) as a postdoctoral research associate. Currently he is a principal researcher at Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI) since December 2007. He currently serves as an Editor-in-Chief of the Journal of Analytical Science & Technology.

Suresh Kumar Kailasa completed his Ph.D. in chemistry from S. V. University, Tirupati in 2005. After completing his two postdoctoral fellowships at South Korea and at National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan, he joined as a faculty at Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat in 2009. He received Young Scientist Award from Taiwan Mass Spectrometry Society in 2013. He worked as Visiting Scientist at Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan. He has selected as Brain pool Scientist at Department of Chemistry, Chung-Ang University, South Korea under Korean Brain Pool Invitation Program of KOFST in 2017. He is the author of 180 peer-reviewed papers and is co-inventor of on Taiwan Patent. His interest includes functional nanomaterials, nanosensors, photonics, biosensors, drug delivery and mass spectrometry.

Tae Jung Park received his Ph.D. degree at the Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering of KAIST in 2004. He has been a professor with distinguished scholarship at the Chemistry department of Chung-Ang University. His research interests are novel platform technologies for nanobio-fusion studies on metal surfaces, biosensors, and nanocomplex fabrication for electrochemical and optical analyses. Furthermore, he is interested in the investigation of molecular- and immune-diagnostics using nanomaterials and the evaluation of biomimetics and drug delivery systems. He has received over 30 research awards, published over 350 papers, and holds over 50 international and 120 Korean patents.
